questions.q: What is the main disadvantage of session-based authentication compared to token-based authentication?
options: Sessions are less secure than tokens
Sessions require server-side storage and don't scale well horizontally
Tokens can never be revoked once issued
Sessions expose the user password in every request
questions.explanation: Session-based auth requires the server to store session state, making horizontal scaling difficult because requests must route to the server that created the session. Token-based auth is stateless. Tokens can be revoked via blacklists; passwords aren't exposed; tokens aren't inherently more secure than sessions.

questions.q: In JWT (JSON Web Token), what does the signature portion verify?
options: The identity of the token issuer and that the payload hasn't been tampered with
The expiration time of the token
The user's password hash
The user's role and permissions
questions.explanation: The JWT signature (created using the secret key and header+payload) proves the token came from a trusted issuer and hasn't been modified. Expiration is a claim in the payload; passwords aren't stored in JWTs; roles/permissions are optional claims but not verified by the signature itself.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the 'refresh token' pattern in OAuth2?
options: To store user permissions and roles
To reduce how often the user must provide credentials while limiting exposure of long-lived access tokens
To encrypt the access token in transit
To verify the client application's identity
questions.explanation: Refresh tokens are long-lived tokens used to obtain new short-lived access tokens without re-authenticating. This balances security (access tokens expire quickly) with user experience (no frequent logins). They don't store permissions, encrypt access tokens, or verify clients (client credentials do that).

questions.q: Which password hashing algorithm is considered secure for storing passwords in 2025?
options: MD5
SHA-1 with multiple iterations
bcrypt with a work factor of at least 12
Simple SHA-256 hashing
questions.explanation: bcrypt with adequate work factors is designed specifically for passwords and includes salt automatically, resisting brute force and GPU attacks. MD5 and SHA-1 are cryptographically broken; SHA-256 alone doesn't include salt and is too fast for password storage. Modern alternatives include Argon2.

questions.q: In a Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) system, what does TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) require?
options: The user's fingerprint via biometric hardware
A time-synchronized secret key and the current time
An SMS sent to the user's phone number
A hardware security key connected to the computer
questions.explanation: TOTP generates codes using a shared secret key and the current time, syncing between client and server. It doesn't require fingerprints, SMS (that's SMSOTP), or hardware keys (that's U2F/WebAuthn). TOTP is popular because it works offline and needs only a standard authenticator app.

questions.q: What vulnerability does Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) exploit in session-based authentication?
options: Weak password requirements
The browser automatically sending cookies with requests to any origin
SQL injection in login forms
Unencrypted session tokens in transit
questions.explanation: CSRF exploits that browsers automatically include session cookies in requests to any origin, allowing attackers to trick authenticated users into unwanted actions. CSRF is prevented via CSRF tokens or SameSite cookies. Password strength, SQL injection, and encryption are separate concerns.

questions.q: In OAuth2, what role does the Authorization Server play?
options: Stores user data and serves protected resources
Issues access tokens after verifying the user's identity and getting consent
Redirects the user to the resource server to fetch data
Acts as the client application requesting access on behalf of the user
questions.explanation: The Authorization Server authenticates users, obtains their consent, and issues access tokens. The Resource Server holds protected data; the Client app requests access; users are redirected during the flow, but the auth server makes the token decision.

questions.q: What is the primary security risk of storing passwords in plain text?
options: It makes the application slower to authenticate users
If the database is compromised, attackers gain immediate access to all user accounts across services
It increases the size of the database
It prevents users from resetting forgotten passwords
questions.explanation: Plain text passwords mean a database breach directly compromises all user accounts, especially if passwords are reused across services. Performance isn't affected; database size is negligible; password resets are unrelated. This is why hashing is mandatory.

questions.q: When should you use API keys for authentication instead of OAuth2?
options: When the API user is a human end-user requiring secure delegation
When the client is a trusted server application and resource scoping isn't needed
When you need to support multiple authentication methods
When the API must be public and unauthenticated
questions.explanation: API keys suit server-to-server, trusted client scenarios where scoping and user consent aren't requirements. API keys are not suitable for delegating human user access (OAuth2) or public/unauth APIs. Multiple auth methods require implementing multiple systems, not choosing one.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the 'nonce' parameter in OAuth2/OpenID Connect?
options: To prevent token replay attacks and ensure the response hasn't been intercepted
To store the user's nickname and account preferences
To encrypt the authorization code
To set the lifetime duration of the access token
questions.explanation: A nonce is a random value the client includes in the request and expects in the response, preventing replay attacks and ensuring responses match requests. It doesn't relate to user metadata, encryption, or token lifetime. It's particularly important in OpenID Connect for securing the implicit flow.
